chapters.title: The troubling state of hillbilly culture today
chapters.text:
When you picture the American dream, a clear image appears: people who started with nothing and built a life through grit and steady work. The story says that with enough effort and a willingness to pull yourself up, you can become anything in America.

The truth is that the American dream is not within reach for everyone. J.D. Vance argues that even people who manage to live the dream carry the weight of the past they left behind.

Hillbillies are a group of Scots-Irish Americans who live mostly in the Appalachian Mountains, from Alabama and Georgia in the South to Ohio and parts of New York in the North. Vance describes Appalachia as a center of misery where the American dream and hope are dimmest, marked by poverty, divorce, and limited social mobility.

This Scots-Irish American group holds tight to its traditions, which have barely changed in centuries. The strong love of culture comes with good traits, like loyalty and devotion to family and country, but it also brings rough edges. They tend to distrust anyone who does not share their look, behavior, or speech. A hillbilly, for example, will find it hard to trust someone who does not match the familiar pattern.

This book offers a firsthand look at the rough spot working-class white Americans find themselves in. It shows how hillbilly culture and the unwritten rules of the community stand in the way of moving up.

For the Scots-Irish to thrive in a fast-changing country and economy, the culture has to change.

chapters.title: The hillbilly mark is hard to shake
chapters.text:
J.D. Vance is a politician from hillbilly culture who made the American dream his own. He left behind a life of violence, drug abuse, and poverty to become a noted lawyer, writer, and politician. He started by tracing his roots. His grandparents, whom he calls Mamaw and Papaw, Jim Vance and Bonnie Blanton, were born and raised in Jackson, Kentucky. They left Jackson for Middletown, Ohio, in the 1940s after Mamaw became pregnant as a teenager and gave birth to a baby girl who died days later.

Middletown was a steel town with better pay than the coal mines of Jackson. Mamaw and Papaw earned a steady income and settled into a middle-class neighborhood, but the mark of being hillbillies never left them. The established white neighbors saw the newcomers as odd and out of place.

Most parts of hillbilly culture drew sharp criticism from long-time Middletown residents. The hillbillies were seen as violent, with too many kids, and with extended family members staying too long in their homes.

Vance's grandparents had three children, Jimmy, Bev, Vance's mother, and Lori, whom he calls Aunt Wee. By the time Jimmy was born in 1951, the family had settled into a working-class life in a thriving neighborhood.

The family fell apart when Papaw started drinking. On the surface, the Vances looked middle class, but at home Papaw terrorized the family. He grew violent and had affairs.

Mamaw found her own way to fight back. In one episode, she soaked her passed-out husband in gasoline and set him on fire. Luckily, one of the kids smothered the flames in time.

Even when you leave your culture behind, life in a new place can be just as harsh.

Mamaw and Papaw split up but stayed close and never divorced on paper. After Papaw moved out, he stopped drinking and tried to make up for the years of harm.

chapters.title: Vance's childhood and teen years
chapters.text:
The violence at home pushed the kids out of the house early. Some of them broke free from the family pattern and built solid lives.

Jimmy worked his way up and became a salesperson at Johnson & Johnson, the first person in the Vance family to hold what could be called a career. At sixteen, Lori dropped out of high school and ended up in a rough marriage. The family helped her leave, and she later married a kind man and built a career in radiology.

Bev, Vance's mother, did not fare as well. Like many children who grew up in violent homes, she carried the scars of her childhood, and she passed some of those scars on to her two children, Lindsay and J.D. Mamaw and Papaw put her through nursing school, but Bev could not hold a job. She slipped back into addiction, bounced through rehab programs, and cycled through marriages.

J.D. Vance was born in 1984 in the Rust Belt town of Middletown, Ohio. Middletown was a thriving working-class community when he was young, but it slid downhill during his childhood. Poverty and crime climbed, and home values fell, which trapped owners who could not afford to move. Armco, a steel company and the town's largest employer, moved most of its jobs overseas, and unemployment soared.

A parent's violence and inability to keep steady, loving relationships leaves a mark on children that follows them their whole lives.

Vance's father, Don Bowman, married Bev after she split from Lindsay's father, her high school boyfriend she had married at nineteen. That marriage fell apart too, and Bev left when J.D. was small. The only father figures he knew were his mother's rotating boyfriends. He started slipping in school and nearly got expelled from high school.

chapters.title: Determination and hard work always pay off
chapters.text:
In his sophomore year of high school, Vance moved in with Mamaw and finally left behind the chaos of living with his mother and her partners.

Living with Mamaw brought new hope and order, but she was not easy. She was sharp-tongued and had no patience for laziness. If he did not clean his room or take out the trash, she would tell him to stop being a lazy piece of nothing. If he skipped homework, she called him out and reminded him he would amount to nothing if he did not study.

Mamaw set three rules for Vance. Get good grades. Get a job. Help around the house. He had to pitch in with whatever she was doing, and she called him out when he slacked. Those three rules gave him structure and helped him become more focused.

Vance wrote that the best part of living with Mamaw was starting to understand what made her tick.

Even with tight money, Mamaw bought him an expensive graphing calculator when he got into the honors Advanced Math class. Her sacrifice pushed him to take school more seriously.

If his grandmother could spend that much on a calculator, he owed it to her to work harder in school. He put in more effort and went on to ace the SAT.

Gratitude can be a powerful motivator.

Mamaw also pushed him to get a job as a cashier at Dillman's, a local grocery store, because she wanted him to learn the value of a dollar. Experience matters, even at jobs you do not love. Low-paid work gets a bad rap it does not deserve, and it can teach skills that matter. Society also depends on workers who get too little credit.

chapters.title: What it takes to feel sure of yourself and succeed
chapters.text:
Vance joined the U.S. Marines right after high school. At first, his family tried to talk him out of it and suggested he go straight to college since his grades could get him in. They tried to convince him the Marines were not for him, but he would not budge. Mamaw tried every angle to change his mind, telling him he was an idiot and the Marines would break him, that he was too dumb for the Corps, and asking who would take care of her.

When the family saw how set he was, they came around. Mamaw took a little longer, but she finally accepted the idea.

Letters from his family carried him through the physical and emotional grind of boot camp. The daily notes of support, especially from Mamaw, showed him how much he was loved. Given the violent and unstable home he had grown up in, feeling loved that deeply meant everything.

Serving in the Marine Corps shaped him up physically, and the experience opened his eyes in ways that changed his life. He learned a lot about himself and his culture, and he met people from every kind of background. His boot camp platoon included Black, white, and Hispanic recruits. There were rich kids, poor kids, Catholics, Jews, and atheists.

The military teaches valuable lessons, like building leadership, managing money well, and growing a sense of gratitude.

Vance's grandmother, Mamaw, died of a complex lung infection in 2005. The whole family was at the hospital when she passed. Vance had always seen Mamaw as the best thing that had ever happened to him. She was his biggest champion. Her loss was almost too much to bear. Still, he took comfort in knowing she had given him what he needed to keep going.

After his service ended in 2007, Vance enrolled at Ohio State University. He worked harder than ever, the way Mamaw had always taught him, and he graduated summa cum laude in under two years.

chapters.title: Climbing into a higher social class
chapters.text:
Once he had his diploma, Vance sent applications to a handful of elite law schools, with Yale at the very top. In 2010, he learned he had been accepted into Yale Law's entering class for 2013. He could not cover tuition on his own, and he was bracing himself for student loans north of two hundred thousand dollars.

Yale Law stepped in with a hefty aid package built around his financial need, freeing up tens of thousands of dollars. For once in his life, having no money actually helped. He had made it into the Ivy League school he had been dreaming of, and the bill for attending came to almost nothing.

Every win in life has a price tag attached.

The moment he walked the campus, the architecture and the long history took his breath away. The other students caught him off guard too. In his very first week, former British prime minister Tony Blair stopped by to deliver a talk. A few days after that, he bumped into New York governor George Pataki.

On the academic side, he was able to keep pace with his classmates. He held his ground in class discussions and on exams. One hard-to-please professor even singled out his writing as impressive.

Even so, Yale never quite felt like home. Roughly ninety-five percent of his classmates came from wealthy or upper-middle-class households, and nearly all of them expected to pull in salaries above $160,000 once they had their law degree. A few of them even worried that a paycheck that high might not be enough. Back in Middletown, no one he knew could imagine earning that kind of money in a single year. The life stretching out in front of him looked nothing like the one he had grown up in.

Vance also wrestled with guilt about leaving his roots behind. He knew that hillbillies tended to look down on the elite, so he could not admit openly back in Middletown that he was now an Ivy League student heading toward a desk job. To Vance, that is the quiet tax that comes with moving up the ladder.

chapters.title: The deep cost of not knowing the rules
chapters.text:
At Yale Law, Vance learned that a resume or an interview does not carry the day in the white-collar world. Social capital, meaning the people around you, is what matters. He saw the value of the people and institutions that surrounded him. Climbing into a higher social class is a steep climb without a strong network. The upper classes protect their circles, and that makes it hard for newcomers to break in.

Surrounding yourself with the right people opens doors to good information and a steady stream of chances.

Vance notes that people who do not understand social capital are at a real disadvantage. Those who do know how to use it can thrive. He also points out that people from his kind of background rarely move up because they do not know what the elites know.

When he arrived at Yale Law, Vance was running on empty in social capital. Like most hillbillies, he had no influential connections, and his social skills were a work in progress. Even though he did well in class, his wealthy classmates had the upper hand. They knew how to navigate a dinner party and how to win over professors and future employers. He was lucky to make a few well-connected friends who helped him find his footing in the upper-class world.

Vance wrote that at Yale, networking is like the air you breathe, so common that it is easy to miss.

Vance graduated from Yale in 2013 and started a successful legal career soon after. Even though the odds were stacked against him as a poor boy from the Kentucky hills who had been abused by his addict mother and her many husbands, he made it and claimed a piece of the American Dream.

A quick fact: according to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, children with adverse childhood experiences, called ACEs, are more likely to suffer from anxiety, depression, and heart disease.

chapters.title: Final thoughts
chapters.text:
Anyone can climb up in America, no matter who they are or where or how they were raised. J.D. Vance's story shows that there is always hope, and a person can succeed and thrive even in the face of cultural and material poverty.

Vance's childhood was a nightmare. He went through every kind of trauma, from growing up without a steady father figure to being yelled at, threatened, and beaten by his drug-addicted mother.

He was lucky to have the love and support of his maternal grandmother, whom he called Mamaw. She gave him stability at a critical time and taught him the value of hard work and grit. Mamaw believed that a person's future does not have to be locked in by the circumstances of birth, so giving in to apathy or cynicism was never an option for Vance.

After finding professional and personal success, Vance now sees that the culture he grew up in puts children at a real disadvantage. Millions of children from white working-class families are living through the same kind of pain and trauma Vance went through. Few of them will be lucky enough to have a Mamaw who can give them the push and the change in view needed to break the cycle.

A small practice to try.

Did you go through childhood trauma or face something that broke your sense of safety and made you feel helpless as a child? Do not give in to defeat. Heal by taking back control of your life. Look for professional support and find ways to connect with others. Work hard, keep going, and stay open to what comes your way.

Finished reading? Nice work.
